summary: DeVault contrasts the process of building a Slack bot versus an IRC bot to illustrate the superiority of open protocols over proprietary platforms. He walks through Slack's multi-step process involving accounts, web portals, SSL-secured servers, and proprietary APIs, then demonstrates that IRC requires nothing more than a TCP connection and a few plaintext commands. The post was prompted by Slack's decision to disable IRC and XMPP gateways, which DeVault frames as a classic Embrace Extend Extinguish move. He argues that IRC's simplicity, openness, and 20+ year track record make it fundamentally better than walled-garden alternatives.
key_points: Slack's bot creation process requires accounts, web portals, SSL servers, proprietary authentication, and navigating complex API documentation
IRC bots can be built with nothing more than a TCP socket and a few plaintext commands, demonstrable via netcat in a terminal
Slack's shutdown of IRC and XMPP gateways exemplifies the Embrace Extend Extinguish strategy against open protocols
IRC's open standard (RFC 1459) can be read locally and offline, unlike proprietary documentation on corporate websites
Software written for IRC 20+ years ago still works today, while Slack can and will break integrations at will
money_quotes: IRC is one of the simplest protocols out there, and it's dead easy to write a bot for it.
If your programming language can open a TCP socket (it can), then you can use it to write an IRC bot in 2 minutes, flat.
Slack is a walled garden. Their proprietary API is defined by them and only implemented by them.
They can and will shut off parts you depend on (like the IRC+XMPP gateways that were just shut down).
IRC is over 20 years old and software written for it then still works now.

I can just read it locally, offline, in the text editor of my choice, rather than on some annoying website that calls authentication a 'triumphant success' and complains about JavaScript being disabled.
It's ubiquitous and free. Use it!
key_insight: The simplicity and longevity of open protocols like IRC expose the unnecessary complexity and vendor lock-in of proprietary platforms like Slack.
